verses.commentary: Back to the synagogue. A man with a withered hand. Another Sabbath, another test. The stage is set for confrontation.
They watch Jesus, looking for a reason to accuse. No longer seeking truth but building a case. Hearts already decided. Will he heal on the Sabbath?
Jesus calls the man forward. Stand up in front of everyone. No hiding, no private miracle. Jesus makes the issue public. Compassion will not be concealed.

Which is lawful on the Sabbath: to do good or evil, to save life or kill? Jesus reframes the question. Inaction is not neutral. To withhold good when you can give it is evil. To refuse healing is to choose harm. Silence from his opponents. They have no answer.

Jesus looks around in anger, deeply distressed at their stubborn hearts (sklerokardia, σκληροκαρδία). Righteous anger. Grief and indignation mingled. Hard hearts grieve God. He commands, Stretch out your hand. The man obeys, and restoration comes. Faith acts on the word.

Pharisees and Herodians plot to kill Jesus. Bitter enemies unite against a common threat. They discuss murder while accusing Jesus of Sabbath-breaking. Irony: they plan to kill on the day they claim to honor God. Religion kills when it loses love.
Jesus withdraws to the lake. Strategic retreat. His time has not yet come. Crowds follow from Galilee. Ministry continues despite opposition.
People stream from every direction: Judea, Jerusalem, Idumea, beyond the Jordan, Tyre, Sidon. Jews and Gentiles. The kingdom draws all peoples. Geography expands; the movement is becoming a flood.
Jesus asks for a boat. Crowd management. The press of need is overwhelming. The boat creates space, allows teaching, prevents being crushed.
Those with diseases push forward to touch him. Desperation. Faith mixed with superstition. Yet Jesus' power flows even through such grasping faith.
Demons fall before him, cry out You are the Son of God. Spiritual beings confess what humans deny. The unseen realm knows his identity immediately.
Jesus silences the demons. He will not receive testimony from darkness. His identity must be revealed in God's timing, through his own words and works.
Jesus goes up the mountain. Moses received the law on a mountain; Jesus now establishes the new covenant community. He calls those he wanted. Sovereign choice. Election. They come.
He appoints twelve. Echo of the twelve tribes. New Israel formed. Purpose twofold: be with him and be sent out. Relationship precedes mission. Presence, then proclamation.
Authority to drive out demons. Jesus delegates his power. The kingdom advances through authorized representatives.
Simon called Peter (Petros, πέτρος, rock). Jesus renames him. New identity conferred. Names matter; they signal destiny.
James and John called Boanerges, Sons of Thunder. Fiery temperaments (see Luke 9:54). Jesus knows their natures, calls them anyway. He transforms, not sanitizes.
Andrew, Philip, Bartholomew, Matthew, Thomas, James son of Alphaeus, Thaddaeus, Simon the Zealot. Fishermen, tax collectors, revolutionaries. Diverse group. Unlikely coalition united by Jesus.
Judas Iscariot, who betrayed him. Mark names him with the qualifier. Betrayal is known from the start. Even among the chosen, there is a traitor. Election does not guarantee faithfulness.
Crowds so intense Jesus and disciples cannot eat. Relentless demand. Ministry consumes. No rest, no privacy. The cost of kingdom work.
His family says He is out of his mind. Even those closest to him think he has lost reason. The kingdom looks like madness to natural eyes. Misunderstood by his own.
Teachers of the law from Jerusalem deliver their verdict: possessed by Beelzebul, prince of demons. They attribute the Holy Spirit's work to Satan. Blasphemy reversed. The unforgivable sin is near.
Jesus uses parables. How can Satan drive out Satan? Logic dismantles their accusation. The argument is absurd on its face.
A divided kingdom falls. Self-evident principle. No kingdom survives civil war.
A divided house falls. Same truth, smaller scale. Internal conflict guarantees collapse.
If Satan fights himself, his end has come. Jesus turns their logic against them. If he casts out demons by Satan's power, Satan is finished. Checkmate.

No one plunders a strong man's house without first binding him. Jesus has entered Satan's domain and is looting it. Exorcisms prove Satan is bound. The stronger man has come (1:7). The kingdom invades enemy territory.
All sins and blasphemies can be forgiven. Breathtaking grace. Every offense, every slander against God or man, every rebellion, all forgivable.

But blasphemy against the Holy Spirit is eternal sin, never forgiven. The unpardonable sin. Not a momentary doubt or word, but settled, hardened attribution of God's work to Satan. To call light darkness, good evil, the Spirit demonic. The one sin that cannot be forgiven is the refusal to recognize the very agent of forgiveness.

He said this because they claimed he has an impure spirit. Context clarifies. They looked at Jesus' exorcisms, the Spirit's clear work, and called it demonic. This is the line that, once crossed, leaves no path back.
His mother and brothers arrive. Standing outside. They do not come in. Physical and spiritual distance implied.
The crowd tells Jesus: your mother and brothers are outside looking for you. Implied: come out, attend to family obligations. Tradition demands it.
Who are my mother and brothers? Shocking question. Jesus redefines family. Blood gives way to faith.
He looks at those seated around him. Here are my mother and brothers. The circle of disciples, the crowd pressing in. These are family.

Whoever does God's will is my brother and sister and mother. New family formed by obedience. Kingdom relationships supersede biological ones. The family of God is constituted by doing the Father's will. Chapter three escalates: healing provokes murder plots, crowds overwhelm, demons confess, twelve are chosen, family misunderstands, leaders blaspheme, and new kinship is defined. The battle lines are drawn.
